coredata.dc:title: A facile construction of Au nanoparticles stabilized by thermo-responsive polymer-tethered carbon dots for enhanced catalytic performance
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Li
Zhang, Tianyi
Lü, Jianhua
Lü, Changli
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs), the youngest member in the carbon nanomaterial family, have drawn considerable attention due to their interesting optical, physicochemical and electronic properties as well as broad promising applications. Here, we developed a facile and effective strategy for the preparation of Au nanoparticles stabilized by thermo-responsive poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAM) functionalized carbon dots (Au@CD@P) under the gentle water media. The as-designed dopamine(DA)-terminated PNIPAM can be easily anchored to CDs via mussel-inspired chemistry route. Both CD@P and CDs could well stabilize the Au nanoparticles with interesting assembled structure. The as-prepared Au@CD and Au@CD@P nanohybrids with good dispersibility and stability exhibited the intriguing catalytic activity for reduction of p-nitrophenol (p-NP). Especially, Au@CD@P as catalyst also played a switching role in regulating the catalytic rate by temperature. In addition, Au@CD@P exhibited excellent recyclability which may have potential in green chemical industry for developing high-activity catalysts and easy production methods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanoparticles
Carbon dots
Thermo-responsive polymer
Dopamine
Catalytic reduction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of polymer grafted carbon dots (CD@P)
Catalytic reaction of nitrophenols using Au@CD and Au@CD@P as catalysts
Catalytic ability of Au@CD and Au@CD@P for nitrophenols

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21574017 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of CDs (a), PNIPAM (b) and CD@P (c).
XPS spectra for C 1s, N 1s and O 1s peaks of CDs (a–c), C 1s and N 1s peaks of CD@P (d and e), C 1s, O 1s and Au 4f peaks of Au@CD1 (f–h).
XPS spectra for C 1s, N 1s and Au 4f peaks of Au@CD@P1 (a–c).
TEM images (a–c), high-resolution TEM images (d-f) and size-distribution histograms (g–i) of Au@CD1 (a, d, g), Au@CD2 (b, e, h), and Au@CD3 (c, f, i).
TEM and high-resolution TEM images of Au@CD@P1 (a, b), TEM image of Au@CD@P2 (inset is the size-distribution histogram) (c), and high-resolution TEM image of Au@CD@P2 (d).

Successive UV–vis spectra of the NaBH4 reduction of (a) p-NP, (b) m-NP, and (c) o-NP catalyzed by Au@CD@P1. (d) Plots of ln(Ct/C0) versus time for the reduction of three isomeric nitrophenols catalyzed by the Au@CD@P1.
Successive reduction of p-NP using Au@CD@P2 (a), plots of ln (Ct/C0) versus time for Au@CD@P1 and Au@CD@P2 (b).
Absorbance spectra of p-NP solutions containing Au@CD1 (a), Au@CD2 (b), and Au@CD3 (c). (d) Dependence of ln (Ct/C0) on the reaction time.

(a) p-NP concentration versus time plots of the p-NP reduction at different temperatures for Au@CD@P1 catalyst, and (b) the dependence of the apparent rate constant (k) on temperature, and the inset is the temperature dependence of optical transmittance at 550 nm for Au@CD@P1 aqueous solutions.
Reusability of the Au@CD@P1 catalyst in the reduction of p-NP.
General scheme for the synthesis of PNIPAM-DA polymer.
Schematic illustration of the preparation and catalytic application of the Au@CD@P nanohybrid catalyst.

head.ce:title: A facile construction of Au nanoparticles stabilized by thermo-responsive polymer-tethered carbon dots for enhanced catalytic performance

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Au NPs stabilized by thermo-responsive poly(N-Isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAM) anchored carbon dots (Au@CD@P) with highly catalytic activity to reduce different isomeric nitrophenols are fabricated and show excellent recyclability and controllable thermo-responsive catalytic performance.

Carbon dots (CDs), the youngest member in the carbon nanomaterial family, have drawn considerable attention due to their interesting optical, physicochemical and electronic properties as well as broad promising applications. Here, we developed a facile and effective strategy for the preparation of Au nanoparticles stabilized by thermo-responsive poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAM) functionalized carbon dots (Au@CD@P) under the gentle water media. The as-designed dopamine(DA)-terminated PNIPAM can be easily anchored to CDs via mussel-inspired chemistry route. Both CD@P and CDs could well stabilize the Au nanoparticles with interesting assembled structure. The as-prepared Au@CD and Au@CD@P nanohybrids with good dispersibility and stability exhibited the intriguing catalytic activity for reduction of p-nitrophenol (p-NP). Especially, Au@CD@P as catalyst also played a switching role in regulating the catalytic rate by temperature. In addition, Au@CD@P exhibited excellent recyclability which may have potential in green chemical industry for developing high-activity catalysts and easy production methods.

ce:section.ce:para: p-Nitrophenol (p-NP) as one of the most common and stubborn organic pollutants in industrial wastewaters, is harmful for human beings and animals. So p-NP should be converted to its reductive derivative, p-aminophenol (p-AP), a valuable intermediate product used in the manufacture of paracetamol and clofibrate drugs, developing agent, antioxygens, and petroleum additives [1–3]. The catalytic transformation of p-NP into p-AP has been extensively discussed during recent years, and most of these researches were focused on the active noble metal nanoparticles (NPs) such as Ag, Au, Pd, and Pt [3–5]. A lot of literatures have declared the wide applications of the noble metal NPs for both scientific researches and practical applications [6–8]. The noble metal NPs due to their easy preparation, suitable size and shape have been widely applied to enhance surface plasmon resonance (SPR) signals for detecting small molecules in extremely dilute concentration of analyte in many fields like food industry, environmental monitoring, and clinical medicine [9,10]. Besides, the noble metal NPs exhibit attractive catalytic, electronic, magnetic, and optical properties, and have been widely applied in hydrogenation of alkynes [11], CO oxidation [12], catalytic reactions [13,14] and so on. Especially, gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) have been one of the hot topics for their numerous catalytic reactions in recent years. For example, Au NPs@magnetic fibrous silica microspheres were prepared and used as a recyclable and efficient catalyst for the reduction of p-NP [15]. But the metal nanoparticles with highly active surface atoms tend to aggregate severely, resulting in obstacles of the catalytic activity [16]. Thus, improving the colloidal stability of Au NPs is regarded as a tricky problem for their practical applications. At present, a variety of materials have been utilized as supports to stabilize metal NPs by designing core-shell structure nanoparticles or a special carrier. For example, polymers, magnetic materials, carbon materials, and silicon have been reported to support and stabilize the Au NPs [17–20].

Therefore, it is vital to pursue a perfect carbon supporting material for highly active metal NPs. Carbon dots (CDs) as a new carbon nanomaterial with unique chemical properties such as excellent water solubility, chemical inertness, low cytotoxicity, biocompatibility and chemical stability [21], have been been considered as a promising support [22], and above features also endow them with the potential applications in photovoltaic devices, bioimaging and sensing, catalysis, medical diagnosis, and pollutants detection [23,24]. For example, Erwin et al. used CDs as versatile photosensitizers for solar-driven catalysis with redox enzymes [25]. Heteroatom doping and surface passivation also endow CDs with abundant surface functional groups and favorable hydrophilicity to promote the catalytic activity [26]. Among these heteroatoms, the nitrogen element as an ideal dopant coupling with carbon atoms could provide excess electrons injecting into CDs to alter their electronic transport properties [27]. It has been previously reported that CDs can serve as electron donors and structural scaffold directly reducing and stabilizing metal NPs [21]. High-efficiency synthesis of Au NPs at room temperature with CDs as media in poly(ethylene glycol) was also reported [28]. Shi et al. also demonstrated that the core-shell structured Au@CDs with a shell thickness of ca. 2 nm and an average diameter of 24 nm could be fabricated using CDs as the reductant for the synthesis of Au NPs [29].

The stimuli-responsive materials have been proved to be the forefront of scientific and technological innovation, which can undergo a significant macroscopic change in response to a wide range of stimuli, such as redox potentials, pH and temperature etc. Especially, it is well known that the temperature-responsive polymers such as poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAM) can experience a reversible “coil-to-globule” phase transition in water due to the hydrophilic–hydrophobic balance at the lower critical solution temperature (LCST) upon heating [30–33]. PNIPAM can be easily obtained through a reversible addition–fragmentation chain transfer (RAFT) polymerization. The end-groups on the chain transfer agent can be modified by biologically relevant catechol which possesses strong and universal adhesion to various substrates [33–35]. Since the concept of green chemistry approaches was instituted for sustainable development, the green route for synthesizing nanoparticles still faced a challenge. The surface modification inspired by the marine mussel Mytilus edulis has caught researcher’s eyes entitled mussel-inspired chemistry, and the potential applications of these multifunctional materials were also explored in diverse fields [36–39]. Thus, many materials could be decorated by different polymers with catechols as a versatile scaffold through Schiff base condensation/Michael addition reaction [36]. This method of surface functionalization is pretty simple and environmentally friendly compared with carbodiimide chemistry. At the same time, the polymers containing the electron-rich groups such as thiol and amino groups are also able to combine with gold to promote the stabilization of the nanoparticles [21,22]. To the best of our knowledge, the catechol-containing polymers functionalized CDs by mussel-inspired chemistry have not been demonstrated.

S-1-Dodecyl-S'-(α,α'-dimethyl-α“-acetic acid) trithiocarbonate (DDAT) as a chain transfer agent (CTA) was synthesized according to the previous literature [40]. 1-((3,4-Dihydroxyphenethyl) amino)-2- methyl-1-oxopropan-2-yldodecylcarbonotrithioate (DA-RAFT) was prepared according to the previous method [33]. Dopamine hydrochloride (DA), gold (III) chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O, 99%), sodium borohydride (NaBH4) and o-, m- and p-nitrophenols were bought from Shanghai Macklin Reagent Co. Ltd. Citric acid, tri(hydroxymethyl) amino methane hydrochloride (Tris-HMA), N-Isopropylacrylamide (NIPAM) and 2,2′-Azobis(2- methylpropionitrile) (AIBN, 98%) were obtained from Aladdin. NIPAM and AIBN were recrystallized from hexane and ethanol, respectively. HAuCl4·3H2O, NIPAM and AIBN were stored in the dark below 4 °C. Tetrahydrofuran (THF) was purified by refluxing over sodium wire. The ultrapure water was obtained from the Direct-Q water purification system (Millipore, USA). The other reagents such as sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and solvents were analytical grade without further purification.

CDs were synthesized according to a previous method [27]. Briefly, citric acid (2.0 g) and Tris-HMA (0.5 g) were mixed and heated up to 225 °C for 20 min. The molten substance was obtained and its color changed from colorless to orange, which proved the formation of CDs. The mixtures were cooled down to room temperature and 5.0 mL ultrapure water was added to dissolve the solid. Then the mixtures were neutralized by 1.0 M NaOH to pH = 7.0. Finally, the solution was dialyzed with a dialysis bag (molecular weight: 1000 Da) for two days. The resulting solution was dried by lyophilization.

The PNIPAM was synthesized via a typical RAFT polymerization. The monomer NIPAM (1.0 g, 8.84 mmol), DA-RAFT (43.0 mg, 0.088 mmol) and AIBN (4.0 mg, 0.027 mmol) were dissolved in 5.0 mL THF with 50 mL three neck flask. The mixed solution was stirred at 70 °C under nitrogen for 12 h after three freeze-pumpthaw cycles. The product was purified thrice by precipitation into petroleum ether (100 mL) and collected by centrifugation. Finally, 0.8 g of pale yellow powder was obtained. 1H NMR (500 MHz, MeOD, ppm): 7.6–8.0 (b, 1H, CONH CH(CH3)2), 6.5–6.7 (a, 3H, (HO)2C6 H 3 ), 4.0 (c, 1H, CONHCH(CH3)2), 2.34–1.51(e, 7H, COCH CH 2, (HO)2C6H3 CH 2)2, f, 6H, (CH 3)2), 1.21–0.92 (d, 6H, CONHCH(CH 3)2,g, 25H, C12 H 25).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of polymer grafted carbon dots (CD@P)
ce:section.ce:para: For the preparation of CD@P, the PNIPAM (0.15 g) was dissolved in 5.0 mL THF, and 25.0 mL Tris buffer solution (pH = 8.5, 10 mmol) of CDs (4.0 mg mL−1) was dropped into it. Under weak basic condition, the catechol groups at the end of PNIPAM chains were oxidized to the active quinone, which could react with the amine of CDs by Schiff base condensation/Michael addition mechanism. After the reaction solution was stirred for three days at room temperature, the final obtained solution was dialyzed in water using a dialysis bag (35 KDa) for 3 days and then was freeze-dried by a freeze-dryer.

The synthesis of Au@CD nanohybrids was similar to the previous procedure [39]. At room temperature, 0.625 mL of HAuCl4 solution (8.0 mmol L−1) was gradually dropped into 2 mL CD aqueous solution (2.0 mg mL−1) under a strenuous stirring in the dark condition. After 48 h, the solution turned red and then was dialyzed with a dialysis bag (14 KDa) for 12 h. The dialysis solution was diluted to 5.0 mL for reserve. To explore the impact of the mass of CDs on the Au@CD hybrids, three nanohybrids called Au@CD1, Au@CD2, Au@CD3 were also prepared from different mass ratios of HAuCl4: CD = 1:2, 1:5, 1:8 using the same method above.

The two Au@CD@P nanohybrids named as Au@CD@P1 (HAuCl4:CD@P = 1:2) and Au@CD@P2 (HAuCl4:CD@P = 1:0.25) were obtained by in situ reduction method. A typical synthetic procedure for Au@CD@P1 was as follows: a mixture solution of HAuCl4 (0.625 mL, 8.0 mmol L−1) and CD@P (2.0 mL, 2.0 mg mL−1) was mixed under continuous magnetic stirring at room temperature for 2 h. Then, NaBH4 (10 μL, 0.4 g mL−1) was added to the above solution and stirred for 24 h. The obtained solution was purified by a dialysis bag (14 KDa), aged overnight, and then diluted to 5.0 mL for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic reaction of nitrophenols using Au@CD and Au@CD@P as catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic activity of Au@CD and Au@CD@P nanohybrids as catalysts was evaluated using the catalytic reduction reaction of o-, m-, p-nitrophenol (NP). Freshly prepared NaBH4 (1.0 mL, 0.2 M), o-, m- or p-NP (6.5 μL, 0.05 M) and 2.0 mL ultrapure water were injected into a quartz cell. The different ratios Au@CD and Au@CD@P catalysts (20.0 μL, 0.4 mg mL−1) were taken in each case and UV–vis measurements were used to monitor the peak centered at 400 nm indicating the conversion of o-, m- or p-NP to o-, m- or p-AP. To confirm the stabilizing effect of CD@P, the Au@CD@P catalyst was subjected to the same reduction reaction for repeated catalytic cycles. An SYC-15G external circulating water bath was adopted to control the temperature of catalytic systems with a precision about 0.01 °C so as to explore the effect of temperature on the catalytic activity. A quartz cuvette (4.0 mL) with a cap was used as the reactor, and the entire catalytic reaction was performed under a closed atmosphere.

1H NMR spectra of samples were obtained in MeOD from an AVANCE Bruker spectrometer (500 MHz). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was recorded on a BrukerVector-22 FTIR spectrometer from 4000 to 400 cm−1. UV–vis absorption spectra were collected on a SHIMADZU UV-2550 UV–visible spectrophotometer at the range of 200–800 nm. The molecular weight of polymers was measured by gel permeation chromatography (GPC) (Waters Corporation, USA) at a flow rate of 1.0 mL min−1 at 25 °C, using DMF as the eluent, and the number-average molecular weight was counted vs. polystyrene standards. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed on a Perkin-Elmer TGA-2 thermogravimetric analyzer from room temperature to 700 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 under nitrogen atmosphere. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were carried out on a Rigaku D/max-TTR-III diffractometer using Cu Ka radiation (l = 0.15405 nm). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was obtained by a JEM-2100F electron microscope. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed from the surface with a diameter of 500 mm in area by means of a Quantum 2000 spectrometer utilizing non-monochromatized Al Ka excitation radiation. The Au content of the catalysts was estimated by inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICPAES, Thermo Jarrel Ash, Franklin, MA, USA).

DA-RAFT was characterized by 1H NMR (Fig. S1). The characteristic peaks at 6.7–6.9 (a) and 0.8 (g) ppm are assigned to the catechol and methyl protons at the end of DA-RAFT [33]. Catechol-terminated PNIPAM was synthesized via RAFT polymerization using a chain-transfer agent containing the catechol group. The 1H NMR spectrum of PNIPAM verified the successful preparation of the desired polymer (Fig. S2) [33]. The characteristic peaks of PNIPAM segments and catechol group were observed at 6.7 (a), 6.5 (a), 7.6 (b), 8.0 (b) and 4.0 (c) ppm. The number of repeated units of PNIPAM chains was determined to be around 42 for each initiating unit by calculating the relative integral area ratio of the peak between a (the catechol protons on the end group of polymer, (HO)2C6 H 3 ) and c (the methane protons in PNIPAM, CONHCH(CH3)2). And the number average molecular weight of the polymer was estimated to be 4700 and 5500 from the 1H NMR and GPC with a polydispersity index (PDI) 1.21, respectively.

The chemical structures of samples were characterized by FTIR. Fig. 1 a illustrated that CDs have the multiple peaks at 3399, 2970 cm−1 and 1394 cm−1 belonging to the stretching vibrations of OH/NH, CH and CNHC, which verified the successful nitrogen doping in the CDs. Furthermore, the peaks at 1739 and 1450–1650 cm−1 are associated with C
O stretching vibration and skeletal vibrations of the aromatic rings and other characteristic vibrations, CO (1194 cm−1) and CH (1076 cm−1) [27,42]. It can be seen that the CDs are composed of aromatic structure containing plenty of organic groups such as amino and phenol hydroxyl groups on the surface whose functional groups could directly reduce Au3+ to gold nanoparticles [22], and more easily stabilize Au NPs. The characteristic amide I and II bands appeared at 1650 and 1540 cm−1 for PNIPAM [43], and the OH/NH and CH stretching vibration were observed at 3295 and 2973 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 1b). As for the sample CD@P, the double characteristic peaks of the amide I and II bands from PNIPAM can also be clearly observed in Fig. 1c. In addition, the absorption bands at 3292, 2973 and 1740 cm−1 are assigned to the vibration peaks of –OH/–NH, C–H and CO vibration, respectively. The above results confirmed that the PNIPAM chains have been successfully anchored to the surface of CDs (CD@P) via Schiff base condensation/Michael addition reaction [36].

The structural identity and phase purity of the CDs, CD@P, Au@CD1 and Au@CD@P1 were confirmed by the powder X-ray diffraction (XRD). The pure CDs exhibited a broad diffraction peak centered at 23.5° corresponding to the graphitic structure of CDs (Fig. S3a) [27]. The XRD pattern of CD@P showed a broad diffraction band at around 20° due to the amorphous polymer PNIPAM (Fig. S3b and c), suggesting that the PNIPAM was grafted onto the surface of CDs. Fig. S3d and e have four distinct characteristic peaks at 2θ = 38.3°, 44.4°, 64.7° and 78.0° corresponding to the (1 1 1), (2 0 0), (2 2 0) and (3 1 1) planes of Au in Au@CD2 and Au@CD@P1 nanohybrids, demonstrating the formation of Au NPs [41,44].

The chemical properties of different samples were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. There are five peaks centered at 284.6, 285.5, 286.5, 287.9 and 289.2 eV in C 1s spectrum of CDs assigned to CC/CC, CN, CO, CO and OCO (Fig. 2 a). And three peaks at 399.7, 400.7 and 401.8 eV can also be clearly observed in the N1s spectrum of CDs corresponding to theN, NH and NH2 bands, respectively (Fig. 2b). The O 1s spectrum of CDs shows two characteristic oxygen states of CO and COH/COC peaks at 532.0 and 533.5 eV (Fig. 2c). As for CD@P, the C-C/C-N peaks in C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) become dominant contrary to CDs. In the N1s spectrum, the NH band of CD@P becomes stronger in intensity and the –NH2 peak disappears compared with CDs (Fig. 2e), which also indicates that the catechol-terminated PNIPAM has been anchored to the surface of CDs with amino groups by Schiff base condensation/Michael addition reaction [45,46]. In the C 1s spectrum of Au@CD1, the CO, CO, OCO and COH/COC peaks seem to be rather small or even disappear in comparison with the C 1s and O 1s spectrum of pure CDs (Fig. 2f and g). This phenomenon may be ascribed to two reasons as follows: (1) there is an interaction for the p/π orbits of C with d orbits of Au in Au NPs; (2) it may be related to the interaction between the Au NPs and the functional groups on the CDs(COH/COC, CC, CO). Both reasons can explain the formation and the high stability of the Au@CD1 nanohybrid [41]. Furthermore, the presence of Au 4f7/2 (84.1 eV) and Au 4f5/2 (87.7 eV) also confirmed the formation of Au@CD1 nanohybrid (Fig. 2h). The C 1s and N 1s spectra (Fig. 3 a and b) of Au@CD@P1 are identical to that of CD@P. The Au 4f7/2 (84.1 eV) and Au 4f5/2 (87.7 eV) are also visible in Au@CD@P1 nanohybrid, indicating that Au3+ has been reduced to form Au NPs by NaBH4 (Fig. 3c). However, the tiny shift in the Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2 toward higher energy may be due to the exchange of electrons between CD/CD@P and Au NPs further leading to more efficient transfer of electrons from the CD/CD@P to the Au NPs with respect to the earlier reports [47].

The thermal properties of different samples were investigated by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) under a nitrogen atmosphere. As shown in Fig. S4, the evaporation of adsorbed water for different samples resulted in a weight loss below 180 °C. The CDs have an obvious weight loss at 200–400 °C due to the decomposition of oxygen-containing functional groups [48]. The weight percent of PNIPAM grafted on the CDs can be calculated about 22 wt%. In addition, the mass loss of Au@CD@P1 dropped sharply in comparison with that of CD@P, demonstrating the formation of Au NPs. Contrary to the pure CDs, the thermal stability of CD@P and Au@CD@P1 was improved by the polymer grafting.

The microstructures of CDs, Au@CD1 and Au@CD@P1 were examined using TEM. Fig. S5 illustrates that CDs are monodispersed spherical structure and the diameter is about 1.5–5.0 nm. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the TEM images of Au@CD1 revealed plenty of large hexagonal and trigonal plates similar to the previous report [49]. The Au@CD1 also showed the average diameter ca. 37.8 nm with the lattice fringe of 0.24 nm corresponding to the (1 1 1) lattice plane of cubic Au NPs (Fig. 4d) [50]. The CDs can stabilize the Au NPs and prevent their aggregation. To further study the influence of the mass of CDs on the morphologies of Au@CD nanostructures, three different Au@CD samples (Au:CD = 1:2, 1:5, 1:8, mass ratio) were homogeneously dispersed in water as shown in their photographs in Fig. S6a. As for Fig. 4b and c, Au@CD2 and Au@CD3 are uniformly dispersed with the average diameters of 10.4 nm and 9.9 nm and the same the lattice fringe of 0.24 nm with Au@CD1 (Fig. 4e, f). These results imply that as for a constant concentration of HAuCl4, the increasing mass of CDs would result in the formation of the smaller Au NPs, and the hexagonal and trigonal plates become relatively rare [49]. The corresponding size distributions of different Au@CD are given in Fig. 4g–i. When the Au@CD@P1 hybrids were prepared from the excess weight ratio of CD@P and gold precursors (HAuCl4:CD@P = 1:2), the in situ generated Au NPs grew along the polymer chains modified CDs. So the Au@CD@P1 hybrid exhibited the special interconnected chain-like assembly structure (Fig. 5 a, b). This phenomenon may be correlated with many electron-rich atoms or groups such as amide bonds, sulfur, nitrogen and amino groups in the CD@P, which can stabilize gold particles [22]. Especially, the generated Au NPs can be bridged by CD@P through their strong interactions to assemble into the interconnected chain structures [51]. For the Au@CD@P2 hybrid obtained from low weight ratio of CD@P and gold precursors (HAuCl4:CD@P = 1:0.25), the TEM images of Au@CD@P2 provided the mono-disperse spherical particles with an average diameter of 6 nm (Fig. 5c). In addition, the HRTEMs of Au@CD@P1 and Au@CD@P2 showed the distinct lattice fringes of 0.24 nm agreeing with the spacing of the (1 1 1) plane in face-centered cubic Au NPs whose photographs are shown in Fig. S6b. This phenomenon may be due to the fact that Au NPs tended to gather and linked with the same CD@P with the decreasing content of CD@P, causing the formation mono-dispersed spherical particles [51]. So, it can be seen that a given concentration of CD@P with increasing mass of HAuCl4 will result in the larger Au NPs. Moreover, no further aggregation was observed for the aqueous solution of Au@CD2 and Au@CD@P1 with high concentration (0.6 mg mL−1) for three weeks or even longer, confirming the favorable stability and dispersibility (Fig. S6c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic ability of Au@CD and Au@CD@P for nitrophenols
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic ability of Au@CD@P was evaluated by the reduction of nitrophenols (p-NP, o-NP and m-NP) to aminophenols (p-AP, o-AP and m-AP) in the presence of excess NaBH4 as the reducing agent. The color of aqueous solution for p-NP changed from bright yellow to yellow-green immediately after adding the freshly prepared reductant NaBH4 solution (0.2 M) with a peak bathochromic shift from 315 nm to 400 nm (Fig. 6 a). This phenomenon was ascribed to the formation of the oxidant of p-nitrophenolate ions under basic conditions [52]. However, the peak intensity of p-nitrophenolate ions at 400 nm did not have obvious change over time in the absence of catalysts, indicating that the reduction of p-NP to p-AP did not occur only with the presence of BH4−. After introducing the Au@CD@P1 into the catalytic reaction system, the peak intensity at 400 nm gradually decreased with yellow-green color fading, demonstrating that p-NP was reduced to p-AP [53,54]. There were similar changes of m-NP and o-NP for UV–vis spectra (Fig. 6b and c). The conversion process was regarded as a model for studying the reaction kinetics. In addition, the apparent reaction rate constant (k) is irrelevant toNaBH4 owing to the excessive concentration of NaBH4 compared with nitrophenols, so the reduction reaction is believed to follow pseudo-first-order kinetics [47,55]. The rate constants (k) can be calculated from the slope of exponential nature C t / C 0 vs time (min), ln (C t/C 0) = ln (A t/A 0) = −kt. It can be seen from Fig. 6d that the catalytic systems with Au@CD@P1 as catalyst shows the rate constants (k) of 0.761, 0.602 and 0.176 min−1 for m-NP, p-NP and o-NP respectively, corresponding to the descending order: o-NP < p-NP < m-NP, which is in accordance with the previous reports [56,57].

The influence of catalyst dosage for Au@CD@P1 and Au@CD2 on the reduction reaction under a constant volume was also studied, and the results showed that the rate was accelerated with the increase of catalysts as shown in Fig. S8. As a control experiment, the pure CDs and Au NPs obtained from citric acid (CA) reduction (see ESI) were also used as catalysts to reduce the p-NP. The results indicated that the unmodified pure CDs could not catalyze p-NP to p-AP for long time (see Fig. S9), and the pure Au NPs@CA with a diameter of 10.4 nm exhibited a lower catalytic activity (k = 0.134 min−1, TOF = 75 h−1) than Au@CD@P (see in Fig. S10). We also compared the catalytic performance of our catalysts (Au@CD@P1, Au@CD2) with other catalysts as shown in Table S1. By contrast, our catalysts in this study exhibited a higher catalytic activity than those of most other catalysts.

The superior catalytic activity of Au@CD@P1 can be summarized as the following reasons: (1) the synergistic effects between CD@P and Au NPs. CD@P possesses large surface area, which could load large amount of Au NPs providing more catalytic active sites. Meanwhile, the presence of polymers also blocks the accumulation of gold particles [62,63]; (2) CDs as a good electron acceptor and donor material can accelerate the electron transfer between the catalyst and reactants compared with traditional surfactant molecules [22,64]; (3) CD@P possesses abundant hydrophilic groups, such as OH, NH and COOH, and so it can strongly bind with other organic molecule substrates [64]; therefore, CD@P can easily absorb more p-NP on the surface of Au@CD@P1, which not only facilitates the accumulation of p-NP on the surface of the catalyst, but also leads to the efficient contact between them; (4) the interfacial electron distribution and transfer between p-AP and the metal particles also have an effect on the rate of production of active H [46,65]. In addition, Au@CD2 behaved better catalytic activity than Au@CD@P1 in our study, and their TOF values were calculated to be 4162 and 597 h−1, respectively. The reason may be that the CDs on the surface of Au NPs facilitate the electron transfer resulting in the enhanced catalytic rate [62]. Moreover, PNIPAM chains may also hinder the substrate close to the catalyst sites, leading to fall in catalytic activity.

Under thermal stimuli, the position of PNIPAM chains in Au@CD@P1 would be altered with the extension and shrinking of polymer chains [66]. Therefore, the dependence of catalytic behavior for Au@CD@P1 catalyst on the temperature was also studied by utilizing the UV–vis spectra. From Fig. 9 , it can be observed that the reaction rate (k) of Au@CD@P1 catalytic system enhanced when the temperature increased from 25 to 30 °C, which confirmed that the reaction rate could be accelerated by the raising temperature. Arrhenius equation can be used to describe the relationship between the reaction rate and temperature for a common catalyst. Noticeably, the phase transition behavior of PNIPAM chains was accompanied by an evident inflection point of k values within a temperature range 30–35 °C [13]. The lower critical solution temperature (LCST) of Au@CD@P1 is around 37 °C measured by the temperature-dependent transmission at 550 nm as shown in the inset of Fig. 9b. Below the LCST, water is a good solvent for PNIPAM, so that the reactants of p-NP and NaBH4 easily reached the surface of gold particles through the brush layer of PNIPAM to trigger the reduction. By contrary, the hydrophobic PNIPAM, like an obstacle, stopped p-NP and NaBH4 close to the Au NPs resulting in the decrease of k value with the temperature up to 45 °C [67]. Thus, the temperature-responsive PNIPAM chains in Au@CD@P1 catalyst play a role as the switch, which can regulate the reaction rate by the temperature of catalytic systems [45].

The reusability and stability of the Au@CD@P1 catalyst were also investigated, which was subjected to the same reduction reaction for repeated catalytic cycles. Fig. 10 shows that the Au@CD@P1 catalyst preserves high catalytic efficiency till the end of 5th cycles of the reduction reaction owing to the supporting and stabilizing effect of PNIPAM and CDs for Au NPs. The cycle experiments of Au@CD@P1 catalyst is superior to the earlier report [32]. The polymer modified CDs provides favorable events for the emergence of such a great stabilizing effect for metal nanoparticles under our experimental conditions.

In summary, we developed an effective and convenient strategy toward the construction of Au NPs stabilized by thermo-responsive poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAM) anchored carbon dots (Au@CD@P). The Au@CD@P1 nanohybrid with smaller size of Au NPs and special interconnected chain-like assembly structure was more favorable for reduction p-NP. Au@CD@P1 and Au@CD2 nanohybrids with highly dispersibility and stability in water exhibited better catalytic activity for the reduction of p-NP as compared with the previous reported catalysts, which was mainly attributed to that the CD@P/CD as the supporting materials had the synergistic effects with Au nanoparticles. In contrary to typical Au@CD nanohybrids, Au@CD@P1 nanohybrids could act as a temperature responsive switch to modulate the catalytic activity of catalytic systems by changing temperature and also behaved excellent reusability. The as-prepared Au@CD@P nanohybrids with thermo-sensitivity, colloidal stability and recyclability can be applied easily in more general catalytic reaction processes, thus providing promising opportunities for the use in optoelectronic, environment, and biological fields and many other potential applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21574017).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2018.05.157.
